name: Bodyweight Squat
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back as if sitting into a chair, keeping your chest up and core braced. Lower yourself until your thighs are at least parallel to the ground or as deep as your mobility allows without your lower back rounding. Drive through your whole foot to return to the starting position, squeezing your glutes at the top.
feelRight: You should feel the load distributed across your quads, glutes, and adductors, with your core engaged throughout. The descent should feel controlled and the ascent powerful, with your weight centered over your mid-foot.
feelWrong: Letting your knees cave inward places undue stress on the ACL and reduces glute activation. Rising onto your toes or leaning too far forward shifts the load to your lower back and compromises balance.
notes: Lower the eccentric phase to a 3-second count to increase time under tension. If you struggle with depth, widen your stance slightly or hold a counterweight in front of your chest.

name: Push-Up
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, fingers spread, and body forming a straight line from head to heels. Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows at a 45-degree angle to your torso, stopping just before your chest touches the ground. Press through your palms to extend your arms back to the starting position, keeping your core braced and hips from sagging throughout the movement.
feelRight: You should feel the primary effort in your chest, front deltoids, and triceps, with your core working to maintain the plank position. The descent should be smooth and controlled, with your elbows tracking at a consistent angle.
feelWrong: Flaring your elbows out to 90 degrees places excessive stress on the shoulder joint. Letting your hips sag or piking your hips up breaks the rigid bodyline and reduces chest engagement.
notes: For a deeper stretch, elevate your hands on push-up handles or dumbbells. To make the exercise harder, raise your feet on a box or add a slow descent with an explosive ascent.

name: Plank Hold
instructions: Start in a forearm plank position with your elbows directly under your shoulders and your forearms parallel on the floor. Extend your legs behind you and balance on your toes, keeping your body in a perfectly straight line from your ankles to your head. Brace your core as if you were about to be punched, and maintain the position without letting your hips drop or rise. Breathe steadily throughout the hold.
feelRight: You should feel a deep burning sensation across your entire core, especially the deep transverse abdominis, with your shoulders, glutes, and quads all actively engaged. The sensation should be uniform across your midsection with no lower back pain.
feelWrong: Letting your hips sag toward the floor puts the load into your lower back instead of your abs. Holding your breath or tensing your neck and shoulders causes unnecessary fatigue and raises blood pressure.
notes: Aim for 3 sets holding as long as possible with good form. If you can hold for 90+ seconds easily, progress to a side plank, a single-leg plank, or add weight to your back.

name: Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent, feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart, and arms at your sides with palms facing down. Press through your heels to lift your hips toward the ceiling, squeezing your glutes at the top of the movement. Hold the peak contraction for 1-2 seconds, then lower your hips back to the starting position without letting them touch the ground. Keep your core braced and your ribs pulled down throughout the motion.
feelRight: You should feel a strong, targeted contraction in your glutes at the top of each rep, not in your hamstrings or lower back. The movement should feel like you're pressing your pelvis upward rather than lifting with your lower back.
feelWrong: Overextending by pushing the hips too high or hyperextending the lower back shifts the work away from the glutes into the lumbar spine. Driving through your toes instead of your heels disengages the glutes and emphasizes the quads instead.
notes: Place a resistance band just above your knees and press outward against it to increase glute medius activation. For a greater challenge, perform the movement with one leg extended in the air (single-leg glute bridge).

name: Reverse Lunge
instructions: Stand tall with your feet hip-width apart and hands on your hips or held in front of your chest. Take a controlled step backward with your right leg, lowering your hips until both knees are bent to approximately 90 degrees, with your back knee hovering just above the ground. Keep your front shin vertical and your torso upright as you push through the heel of your front foot to return to the starting position. Alternate legs for the prescribed number of reps.
feelRight: You should feel the work concentrated in your front-leg glute and quad, with the back leg providing stability rather than power. The movement should feel balanced and controlled with no lateral shifting of the hips.
feelWrong: Leaning your torso forward or letting your front knee drift past your toes places shearing force on the knee joint. Taking too short a step back prevents you from reaching 90 degrees of knee bend and reduces glute engagement.
notes: The reverse lunge places less stress on the knee than a forward lunge because the tibia stays more vertical. Add a front-foot elevation (standing on a low platform) to increase range of motion.

name: Bear Crawl
instructions: Start on all fours with your wrists under your shoulders and knees under your hips, then lift your knees about one inch off the ground so your weight is supported on your hands and the balls of your feet. Crawl forward by simultaneously moving your right hand and left foot forward, then your left hand and right foot, keeping your back flat and hips low. Move in a controlled, coordinated rhythm without letting your knees or torso touch the ground. Continue for the prescribed distance or duration.
feelRight: You should feel your core working hard to prevent rotation and sagging, with your shoulders, quads, and glutes all actively engaged. The movement should feel stable and rhythmic like a coordinated animal gait.
feelWrong: Letting your hips rise too high turns the crawl into a walking plank and reduces the demand on your core. Crossing your hand and foot paths or moving the same-side hand and foot together makes you unstable and slows you down.
notes: Keep your back as flat as a tabletop throughout — if your hips rise, you're losing core tension. For variety, crawl backward or laterally to challenge the stabilizers differently.

name: Assisted Pull-Up
instructions: Set up a resistance band across the pull-up bar and place a knee or foot in the loop, or use an assisted pull-up machine if available. Grip the bar with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. Hang with your arms fully extended, then pull your chest toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back. Lower yourself with control to the starting position, maintaining tension throughout the full range of motion.
feelRight: You should feel the primary effort in your lats and biceps, with your core engaged to prevent excessive swinging. The band or assist should reduce enough weight that you can complete your reps with consistent form.
feelWrong: Using too much assistance from the band or machine reduces the stimulus and delays progress. Kipping or jerking your body to reach the bar builds momentum rather than strength and can strain the shoulders.
notes: Choose a band thickness that allows you to complete 3 sets of 8-10 with the last 2 reps being challenging. Progress by using a thinner band or increasing reps before moving to unassisted pull-ups.

name: Pull-Up
instructions: Jump up to grip a pull-up bar with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder-width, hanging with your arms fully extended and your body stable. Pull your chest toward the bar by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top. Lower yourself under complete control to full arm extension before beginning the next rep. Avoid kipping or swinging your legs for momentum.
feelRight: You should feel the pull originating from your lats and mid-back, with your biceps assisting as the elbows bend. The movement should feel smooth and powerful with no jerking or uncontrolled descent.
feelWrong: Chin-ups over the bar without full arm extension on the descent shortchanges the range of motion and reduces strength gains. Kipping or using excessive body swing builds momentum but bypasses the strength stimulus in the lats.
notes: If you can't complete a full set, use negatives (jump up and lower for 3-5 seconds) to build strength. Progress to weighted pull-ups with a belt or dip belt once you can do 10-12 clean reps.

name: Parallel Bar Dips
instructions: Grip the parallel bars with your arms straight and lift yourself so your body is supported above the bars. Lean forward slightly and lower your body by bending your elbows until your upper arms are parallel to the ground or your shoulders feel a comfortable stretch. Press through your palms to return to the starting position without locking your elbows at the top. Control the descent and keep your shoulders down away from your ears throughout.
feelRight: You should feel the effort distributed across your chest, front deltoids, and triceps, with your core engaged to keep your body stable. The movement should feel smooth and controlled through a full range of motion.
feelWrong: Descending too low or letting your shoulders roll forward places excessive strain on the shoulder capsule. Flaring your elbows out and keeping your torso too upright shifts the emphasis entirely to the triceps and reduces chest involvement.
notes: Lean your torso forward to target the chest more, or stay upright to isolate the triceps. If you can do 12+ clean reps, add weight using a dip belt or hold a dumbbell between your knees.

name: Bodyweight Row
instructions: Set a barbell in a squat rack at hip height or use a suspension trainer. Lie underneath the bar and grasp it with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, with your body forming a straight line from your heels to your shoulders. Pull your chest toward the bar by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower yourself with control to full arm extension before beginning the next rep.
feelRight: You should feel the contraction deep in your mid-back, rhomboids, and rear deltoids, with your core working constantly to prevent your hips from sagging. The movement should be smooth with no jerking or shrugging at the top.
feelWrong: Collapsing your chest or rounding your shoulders at the bottom shifts the load away from your back. Letting your hips sag or piking them up breaks the rigid bodyline and reduces the pulling stimulus on your lats.
notes: The lower the bar height, the harder the exercise — start with the bar at hip height and lower it as you get stronger. Keep your body rigid like a plank throughout the entire rep.

name: Burpee
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Drop into a squat and place your hands on the floor in front of you. Jump or step your feet back into a plank position, keeping your body in a straight line. Perform a full push-up, then jump or step your feet back to the squat position. Explode upward into a small jump with your arms reaching overhead, then land softly and immediately begin the next rep.
feelRight: You should feel your heart rate spike quickly with the full-body effort distributed across your legs, chest, shoulders, and core. Each rep should flow smoothly from one phase to the next without pausing or resetting.
feelWrong: Letting your hips sag during the plank phase places strain on the lower back. Landing heavily or softly rebounding into the next rep increases impact on your knees and ankles unnecessarily.
notes: For a lower-impact version, step back instead of jumping and eliminate the push-up. If you can do 20+ clean reps, add a tuck jump at the top for explosive power development.

name: Dead Bug
instructions: Lie on your back with your arms extended straight up toward the ceiling and your knees lifted to a tabletop position (hips and knees at 90 degrees). Press your lower back into the floor by engaging your core. Slowly extend your right arm overhead and your left leg toward the ground simultaneously, keeping your lower back pressed flat. Return to the starting position, then repeat with the left arm and right leg, alternating in a slow, controlled rhythm.
feelRight: You should feel your deep core muscles (transverse abdominis) working hard to maintain the flat back position as your limbs move. The movement should feel challenging but controlled, with no arching of the lower back.
feelWrong: Letting your lower back arch off the floor as your limbs extend is the most common mistake — if you feel your back lifting, reduce the range of motion. Rushing through the reps to make them easier negates the core stability benefit.
notes: The closer your extended limbs get to the ground, the more challenging the exercise. Keep your breathing steady and exhale as you extend your limbs for better core engagement.

name: Side Plank
instructions: Lie on your right side with your legs stacked and your right elbow directly under your shoulder, forearm on the floor perpendicular to your body. Lift your hips off the ground so your body forms a straight line from your ankles to your shoulders. Keep your left arm extended toward the ceiling or resting on your hip for stability. Hold the position without letting your hips sag or your top shoulder roll forward.
feelRight: You should feel the oblique muscles on the side of your torso and the gluteus medius of your bottom leg working to keep you lifted. The sensation should be a steady burn through the lateral core musculature, not sharp pain in the shoulder.
feelWrong: Letting your hips drop toward the floor removes tension from the obliques and defeats the purpose of the exercise. Allowing your top shoulder to roll forward or your head to droop compromises the straight line and reduces stability demand.
notes: If holding on your elbow is too intense, perform the side plank from your knees instead. For a harder variation, lift your top leg and hold it in the air, or add a hip dip motion.

name: Mountain Climber
instructions: Start in a high plank position with your hands directly under your shoulders, your body forming a straight line from head to heels. Drive your right knee toward your chest without letting your hips rise, then quickly extend it back and drive your left knee forward. Alternate legs in a running motion while keeping your core braced and your hips as low as possible. Maintain a steady, controlled rhythm throughout the set.
feelRight: You should feel your hip flexors, quads, and core working in coordination, with your shoulders and arms stabilizing the upper body. Your breathing should be elevated but still controlled, matching the rhythm of your knee drives.
feelWrong: Bouncing your hips up toward the ceiling each time you switch legs turns the exercise into a pike position and eliminates core tension. Rushing the cadence at the expense of full hip flexion reduces the range of motion and the stimulus to the hip flexors.
notes: For a slower, more controlled variation, pause for one second with each knee at the chest. To increase difficulty, float your hands on a pair of sliders or a towel on a smooth surface.
